docs.title: Rinsed Snowflake Data Client
docs.text:
Python client for WashU Carwash Rinsed CRM data in Snowflake.

Provides fast, bulk access to historical KPI data via Snowflake — complementing the Sonny's Data API Client which is best suited for real-time data pulls.

docs.title: Why This Client?
docs.text: The Sonny's Data API has strict rate limits (20 requests per 15 seconds). For bulk historical analysis — monthly reports, trend analysis, churn tracking — Snowflake is significantly faster. This client mirrors the Sonny's client patterns (client.stats.*) so the API feels familiar.

docs.title: Quick Start
docs.text:
from rinsed_snowflake_client import RinsedClient

with RinsedClient() as client:
    # Car counts
    cars = client.stats.total_car_count("2026-01-01", "2026-01-31")
    print(f"Total washes: {cars.total}")

    # Revenue
    rev = client.stats.retail_revenue("2026-01-01", "2026-01-31")
    print(f"Retail revenue: ${rev.total:,.2f}")

    # Conversion rate
    conv = client.stats.conversion_rate("2026-01-01", "2026-01-31")
    print(f"Conversion: {conv.rate:.1%}")

    # All KPIs at once
    report = client.stats.report("2026-01-01", "2026-01-31")

docs.title: Installation
docs.text:
pip install rinsed-snowflake-client

Or from source:

git clone https://github.com/christopher-nance/Rinsed-Snowflake-Data-Client.git
cd Rinsed-Snowflake-Data-Client
pip install -e ".[dev]"

docs.title: Available KPIs
docs.text:
All methods are on client.stats and accept start, end, and optional locations:

Method Returns Description total_car_count() CarCountResult Total washes (retail + member + free) retail_car_count() CarCountResult Retail (non-member) wash count member_car_count() CarCountResult Membership redemption count retail_revenue() RevenueResult Revenue from retail washes membership_revenue() MembershipRevenueResult Revenue from memberships (new + renewed) average_wash_price() AWPResult Retail ticket average new_membership_sales() MembershipSalesResult New + rejoin membership count conversion_rate() ConversionResult Sales / eligible washes voluntary_churn_rate() ChurnResult Member-initiated cancellations involuntary_churn_rate() ChurnResult Expired / failed payment churn report() StatsReport All KPIs bundled

docs.title: rinsed_snowflake_client.RinsedClient(account: str | None = None, user: str | None = None, password: str | None = None, warehouse: str | None = None, database: str | None = None, schema: str | None = None, role: str | None = None)
docs.text:
Client for WashU Rinsed Snowflake data.

Parameters:

Name Type Description Default account str | None

Snowflake account identifier. If None, loads from env.

None user str | None

Snowflake username.

None password str | None

Snowflake password.

None warehouse str | None

Snowflake warehouse name.

None database str | None

Snowflake database name.

None schema str | None

Snowflake schema name.

None role str | None

Optional Snowflake role.

None

Examples:

docs.title: From environment variables
docs.text: with RinsedClient() as client: result = client.stats.total_car_count("2026-01-01", "2026-01-31") print(result.total)

docs.title: Explicit credentials
docs.text: client = RinsedClient(account="...", user="...", password="...", warehouse="...", database="...", schema="...") client.connect() df = client.query("SELECT * FROM conversion_daily LIMIT 10") client.close()

Power-user escape hatch for ad-hoc queries.

required params Sequence | None

docs.title: rinsed_snowflake_client.resources.StatsResource(client: RinsedClient)
docs.text:
Analytics and KPI methods.

Access via client.stats.

docs.title: average_wash_price(start: DateInput, end: DateInput, locations: Locations = None) -> AWPResult
docs.text: Average Wash Price = retail revenue / retail car count.

docs.title: conversion_rate(start: DateInput, end: DateInput, locations: Locations = None) -> ConversionResult
docs.text: Conversion rate = sales / eligible washes (from CONVERSION_DAILY).

docs.title: involuntary_churn_rate(start: DateInput, end: DateInput, locations: Locations = None) -> ChurnResult
docs.text: Involuntary churn rate (expired memberships / failed payments).

docs.title: member_car_count(start: DateInput, end: DateInput, locations: Locations = None) -> CarCountResult
docs.text: Membership redemption (member wash) count.

docs.title: membership_revenue(start: DateInput, end: DateInput, locations: Locations = None) -> MembershipRevenueResult
docs.text: Revenue from new + renewed memberships.

docs.title: new_membership_sales(start: DateInput, end: DateInput, locations: Locations = None) -> MembershipSalesResult
docs.text: Count of new + rejoin membership sales.

docs.title: report(start: DateInput, end: DateInput, locations: Locations = None) -> StatsReport
docs.text: All KPIs in a single call.

docs.title: retail_car_count(start: DateInput, end: DateInput, locations: Locations = None) -> CarCountResult
docs.text: Retail (non-member) wash count.

docs.title: retail_revenue(start: DateInput, end: DateInput, locations: Locations = None) -> RevenueResult
docs.text: Revenue from retail (non-member) washes.

docs.title: total_car_count(start: DateInput, end: DateInput, locations: Locations = None) -> CarCountResult
docs.text: Total washes (retail + member + free) from CONVERSION_DAILY.

docs.title: voluntary_churn_rate(start: DateInput, end: DateInput, locations: Locations = None) -> ChurnResult
docs.text: Voluntary churn rate (member-initiated cancellations).

docs.title: Exceptions
docs.text:
All exceptions inherit from RinsedError, allowing you to catch all client errors with a single handler:

from rinsed_snowflake_client import RinsedClient, RinsedError

try:
    with RinsedClient() as client:
        result = client.stats.total_car_count("2026-01-01", "2026-01-31")
except RinsedError as e:
    print(f"Client error: {e}")

docs.title: Exception Hierarchy
docs.text:
RinsedError
├── ConfigurationError
├── ConnectionError
├── QueryError
└── ValidationError

docs.title: WashU Churn Definition
docs.text:
This client uses WashU's billing-cycle churn definition, which differs from Rinsed's real-time recording:

  • Rinsed records churn on the day the cancellation or expiry happens
  • WashU recognizes churn when the would-be recharge doesn't process (one billing cycle later)

Example: A member buys on 1/12, gets recharged on 2/12, then cancels on 2/23. Rinsed records the churn in February. WashU reports it in March — when the 3/12 recharge would have occurred but didn't, since the member cancelled.

The rationale: a member who was billed for the month has already paid, so they're still a member for that month. Their departure is recognized the following month.

Info

WashU's churn numbers for any given month are equivalent to Rinsed's churn numbers from the previous month, shifted forward by one billing cycle.

docs.title: Voluntary Churn
docs.text:
Member-initiated cancellations (terminated in Rinsed).

result = client.stats.voluntary_churn_rate("2026-02-01", "2026-02-28")
print(f"Rate: {result.rate:.2%}")
print(f"Churned: {result.churned_count:,}")
print(f"Starting members: {result.starting_count:,}")

docs.title: Involuntary Churn
docs.text:
Expired memberships due to failed payments (expired in Rinsed).

result = client.stats.involuntary_churn_rate("2026-02-01", "2026-02-28")
print(f"Rate: {result.rate:.2%}")
print(f"Churned: {result.churned_count:,}")

docs.title: Per-Location Breakdown
docs.text:
Both churn methods include a by_location breakdown with per-location churn rates:

result = client.stats.voluntary_churn_rate("2026-02-01", "2026-02-28")

for loc in result.by_location:
    print(f"{loc.location_name}: {loc.value:.2%}")

docs.title: How It's Calculated
docs.text:
Numerator: Count of distinct members from MEMBER_HISTORY whose last billing (created_month) was in the month before the requested period and who have a churn record.

Denominator: Active members from ACTIVE_MEMBERS_MONTHLY (Rinsed definition) for the month before the requested period.

Rate: churned_count / starting_count

docs.title: Comparing to Rinsed
docs.text:
Since the WashU definition is Rinsed's numbers shifted by one month:

WashU Month Equals Rinsed Month February 2026 churn January 2026 churn March 2026 churn February 2026 churn April 2026 churn March 2026 churn

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text: The client needs Snowflake credentials. Set them as environment variables or pass them directly.

docs.title: Environment Variables
docs.text:
Create a .env file (see .env.example):

SNOWFLAKE_ACCOUNT=your_account_identifier
SNOWFLAKE_USER=your_username
SNOWFLAKE_PASSWORD=your_password
SNOWFLAKE_WAREHOUSE=your_warehouse
SNOWFLAKE_DATABASE=your_database
SNOWFLAKE_SCHEMA=your_schema
SNOWFLAKE_ROLE=your_role  # optional

Load with python-dotenv before creating the client:

from dotenv import load_dotenv
load_dotenv()

from rinsed_snowflake_client import RinsedClient

with RinsedClient() as client:
    result = client.stats.total_car_count("2026-01-01", "2026-01-31")

docs.title: Explicit Credentials
docs.text:
client = RinsedClient(
    account="your_account",
    user="your_user",
    password="your_password",
    warehouse="your_warehouse",
    database="your_database",
    schema="your_schema",
    role="your_role",  # optional
)
client.connect()
# ... use client ...
client.close()

docs.title: Context Manager
docs.text:
The recommended pattern is to use RinsedClient as a context manager, which automatically connects and closes the Snowflake connection:

with RinsedClient() as client:
    cars = client.stats.total_car_count("2026-01-01", "2026-01-31")
    rev = client.stats.retail_revenue("2026-01-01", "2026-01-31")

docs.title: Date Parameters
docs.text:
All KPI methods accept start and end dates as either:

  • ISO format strings: "2026-01-01"
  • datetime objects: datetime(2026, 1, 1)
from datetime import datetime

# String dates
result = client.stats.total_car_count("2026-01-01", "2026-01-31")

# datetime objects
result = client.stats.total_car_count(
    datetime(2026, 1, 1),
    datetime(2026, 1, 31),
)

docs.title: Location Filtering
docs.text:
All KPI methods accept an optional locations parameter:

# All locations (default)
client.stats.total_car_count("2026-01-01", "2026-01-31")

# Single location
client.stats.total_car_count("2026-01-01", "2026-01-31", locations="Burbank")

# Multiple locations
client.stats.total_car_count(
    "2026-01-01", "2026-01-31",
    locations=["Burbank", "Carol Stream", "Des Plaines"],
)

Note

Hub Office and Query Server locations are always excluded from results.

docs.title: Result Objects
docs.text:
All KPI methods return typed Pydantic models. Most include a scalar total plus a per-location breakdown:

result = client.stats.total_car_count("2026-01-01", "2026-01-31")

# Aggregate total
print(result.total)  # 288389

# Per-location breakdown
for loc in result.by_location:
    print(f"{loc.location_name}: {loc.value}")

# Period metadata
print(result.period_start)  # "2026-01-01"
print(result.period_end)    # "2026-01-31"

docs.title: Raw Queries
docs.text: For ad-hoc analysis beyond the built-in KPI methods, use client.query() to execute raw SQL against Snowflake. This returns a pandas DataFrame.

docs.title: Basic Usage
docs.text:
with RinsedClient() as client:
    df = client.query("SELECT * FROM conversion_daily LIMIT 10")
    print(df)

docs.title: Parameterized Queries
docs.text:
Use %s placeholders for safe parameterized queries:

df = client.query(
    "SELECT * FROM fct_revenue WHERE location_name = %s AND DATE(created_at) = %s",
    ["Burbank", "2026-03-01"],
)

docs.title: Available Tables
docs.text:
Key tables in the WASHU_RINSED_SHARE.MB schema:

Table Description FCT_REVENUE All revenue transactions (retail washes, memberships, combos) FCT_WASHES Wash records with categories: base wash, redemption, free wash FCT_MEMBERSHIPS Membership billing (new, renewed, rejoin) FCT_REDEMPTIONS Membership wash redemptions CONVERSION_DAILY Pre-aggregated daily conversion metrics CONVERSION_HOURLY Pre-aggregated hourly conversion metrics CONVERSION_SHIFT Pre-aggregated shift-level (AM/PM) metrics MEMBER_HISTORY Member lifecycle with churn type and dates ACTIVE_MEMBERS_MONTHLY Monthly active member counts by location

docs.title: Schema Discovery
docs.text:
# List all tables
df = client.query("SHOW TABLES")

# Inspect a table's columns
df = client.query("SHOW COLUMNS IN TABLE fct_revenue")

# Sample data
df = client.query("SELECT * FROM fct_revenue LIMIT 5")

Warning

The query() method executes arbitrary SQL. Only use SELECT statements — never write to the database.

docs.title: Stats & KPIs
docs.text: All KPI methods are accessed via client.stats.

docs.title: Total Car Count
docs.text:
Total washes across all types (retail + member + free), sourced from CONVERSION_DAILY.

result = client.stats.total_car_count("2026-02-01", "2026-02-28")
print(f"Total washes: {result.total:,}")

docs.title: Retail Car Count
docs.text:
Non-member, paid wash transactions from FCT_REVENUE. Excludes NM&R and RM&R combo transactions (those are membership washes).

result = client.stats.retail_car_count("2026-02-01", "2026-02-28")
print(f"Retail washes: {result.total:,}")

docs.title: Member Car Count
docs.text:
Membership redemptions (member scanned their unlimited pass) from FCT_WASHES.

result = client.stats.member_car_count("2026-02-01", "2026-02-28")
print(f"Member washes: {result.total:,}")

docs.title: Retail Revenue
docs.text:
Revenue from retail (non-member) wash transactions only. NM&R and RM&R combo revenue is excluded — those dollars are membership revenue.

result = client.stats.retail_revenue("2026-02-01", "2026-02-28")
print(f"Revenue: ${result.total:,.2f}")
print(f"Transactions: {result.transaction_count:,}")

docs.title: Membership Revenue
docs.text:
Revenue from new + renewed membership billing, with a breakdown:

result = client.stats.membership_revenue("2026-02-01", "2026-02-28")
print(f"Total: ${result.total:,.2f}")
print(f"New member revenue: ${result.new_revenue:,.2f}")
print(f"Renewal revenue: ${result.renewal_revenue:,.2f}")

docs.title: Average Wash Price (AWP)
docs.text:
Retail ticket average: retail_revenue / retail_car_count.

result = client.stats.average_wash_price("2026-02-01", "2026-02-28")
print(f"AWP: ${result.awp:.2f}")

docs.title: New Membership Sales
docs.text:
Count of new + rejoin membership transactions from FCT_MEMBERSHIPS.

result = client.stats.new_membership_sales("2026-02-01", "2026-02-28")
print(f"New members: {result.total:,}")
print(f"Revenue: ${result.total_revenue:,.2f}")

docs.title: Conversion Rate
docs.text:
Sales / eligible washes, sourced from the pre-aggregated CONVERSION_DAILY table.

result = client.stats.conversion_rate("2026-02-01", "2026-02-28")
print(f"Rate: {result.rate:.1%}")
print(f"Sales: {result.sales:,}")
print(f"Eligible: {result.eligible_washes:,}")

# Per-location conversion rates
for loc in result.by_location:
    print(f"  {loc.location_name}: {loc.value:.1%}")

docs.title: Bundled Report
docs.text:
Get all KPIs in a single call:

report = client.stats.report("2026-02-01", "2026-02-28")

print(f"Total cars: {report.total_car_count.total:,}")
print(f"Retail cars: {report.retail_car_count.total:,}")
print(f"Member cars: {report.member_car_count.total:,}")
print(f"Retail revenue: ${report.retail_revenue.total:,.2f}")
print(f"Membership revenue: ${report.membership_revenue.total:,.2f}")
print(f"AWP: ${report.average_wash_price.awp:.2f}")
print(f"New sales: {report.new_membership_sales.total:,}")
print(f"Conversion: {report.conversion.rate:.1%}")

Tip

report() makes multiple Snowflake queries internally. For dashboards where you need all KPIs, this is more convenient than calling each method individually.
